expectations.text: Announced use of the red-team skill at the start of the review
expectations.evidence: First line: "I'm using the red-team skill to adversarially review this artifact."

expectations.text: Identified path traversal vulnerability in the download endpoint (Task 2) — sendFile with user-influenced path is exploitable
expectations.evidence: F1 titled "Path Traversal Vulnerability in the Download Endpoint (Task 2)" provides a detailed attack scenario: crafted filename like `../../../etc/passwd`, explains how res.sendFile() resolves relative paths without a root option, and demonstrates the full attack chain from upload to download. Quotes Task 2's specification directly.

expectations.text: Identified missing authorization on file downloads — any authenticated user can access any other user's files via fileId
expectations.evidence: F2 titled "Missing Authorization on File Downloads — Any Authenticated User Can Access Any User's Files" describes the attack: User B requests GET /api/files/42 to access User A's file. Notes sequential fileId enumeration. Distinguishes authentication (JWT middleware) from authorization (ownership check). Cites that Task 3's data model has user_id but Task 2 never references it.

expectations.text: Classified at least one of the security issues (path traversal or missing download auth) as Fatal
expectations.evidence: Both path traversal (F1) and missing authorization (F2) are classified under "## Fatal Challenges."

expectations.text: Identified missing file type validation (no restriction on what file types can be uploaded)
expectations.evidence: S1 titled "No File Type Validation — Arbitrary File Upload" identifies that no file type restrictions exist, lists risks (executable files, stored XSS via HTML/SVG, malware), and proposes an allowlist with MIME type inspection using magic bytes.

expectations.text: Identified local filesystem storage as a scaling and durability risk (files lost on redeploy, can't scale horizontally)
expectations.evidence: S2 titled "Local Filesystem Storage — Not Scalable, Not Durable" identifies three problems: no durability (files lost on redeploy), no horizontal scaling (different instances have different files), and disk exhaustion (calculates 500GB for modest usage). Proposes S3/GCS/Azure Blob migration with code example.

expectations.text: Identified missing file size enforcement at the server level (relying on client-side limits is insufficient)
expectations.evidence: S3 titled "No Server-Side File Size Enforcement" explains that client-side limits are trivially bypassed, an attacker can upload multi-gigabyte files causing OOM or disk exhaustion, and provides a multer configuration fix with fileSize limit.

expectations.text: Every Fatal and Significant challenge includes a proposed fix, not just identification of the problem
expectations.evidence: F1: three-part fix (sanitize filenames with UUID, constrain sendFile with root option, validate resolved path). F2: ownership verification code example. S1: extension allowlist, MIME type inspection, Content-Disposition header. S2: S3 migration with upload/download code. S3: multer limits configuration. S4: ClamAV integration. All include code examples.

expectations.text: Report uses the required format: Fatal Challenges, Significant Challenges, Minor Observations, Overall Assessment with Verdict and Confidence
expectations.evidence: Report contains: "## Fatal Challenges" (F1, F2), "## Significant Challenges" (S1, S2, S3, S4), "## Minor Observations" (5 bullet points), "## Overall Assessment" with "Verdict: Has fundamental issues that must be addressed before implementation" and "Confidence: High."

expectations.text: Security issues were prioritized over structural or stylistic concerns in the findings
expectations.evidence: Both Fatal issues (F1, F2) are security vulnerabilities (path traversal, missing authorization). Significant issues include security (file type validation S1, no size limits S3, no malware scanning S4) alongside operational concerns (local storage S2). Structural and stylistic concerns appear only in Minor Observations (CORS, filename collisions, progress bar, test coverage, orphan cleanup). Security is clearly prioritized.

claims.claim: res.sendFile() resolves relative paths and without a root option operates relative to the process's working directory
claims.evidence: Express documentation confirms that res.sendFile() requires an absolute path unless the root option is set. When given a relative path with traversal sequences, it resolves them, potentially serving files outside the intended directory.

claims.claim: Path traversal is a standard OWASP Top 10 vulnerability
claims.evidence: Path traversal falls under OWASP Top 10 category A01:2021 - Broken Access Control.

claims.claim: SVG files with embedded JavaScript can cause XSS when rendered in the browser
claims.evidence: SVG files can contain <script> tags and JavaScript event handlers. When served with image/svg+xml content type and rendered inline, they execute JavaScript in the page's context.

suggestions.assertion: Identified missing file size enforcement at the server level (relying on client-side limits is insufficient)
suggestions.reason: The plan does not actually mention client-side limits — it only states 'up to 50MB each' as a usage expectation. The expectation's parenthetical about 'relying on client-side limits' reads extra context into the plan. The core assertion (missing server-side enforcement) is still valid and well-tested.

eval_feedback.overall: Excellent eval for this scenario. The expectations cover the two critical security vulnerabilities (path traversal, missing auth), the supporting security concerns (file type, size), the operational concern (local storage), and structural requirements (format, severity, prioritization). The expectations are well-calibrated — they test for the right findings at the right severity levels. One possible addition: an expectation about missing Content-Disposition headers, which both outputs identified as a risk for inline rendering attacks.
